Verifier supplementation for ICT__vs__TICT is now sufficient and close-family specific: (i) close-family D–A cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens are consistently assigned ICT (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, RIR/AIE) rather than a stabilized dark TICT minimum; (ii) classic TICT hallmarks require dual emission, viscosity-dependent CT band formation, and an energetically stabilized S1 minimum near ~90° twist; methoxy donors are cited as generally insufficient for stabilized TICT compared with dimethylamino donors; (iii) close-family mechanistic studies report S1 minima at moderate twist (not 90°) and explicitly note no TICT dual-band signature. This external evidence aligns with internal torsion-snapshot results: twisting strongly quenches oscillator strength and shifts the first bright state upward, but the dark S1 is not energetically stabilized with twist (argues against TICT dominance). Overall, verifier evidence is strong enough to materially advance closure and supports ICT over TICT; closure justification for ICT vs TICT is now sufficient for decisive finalization.

final_hypothesis_rationale: Assign ICT as the dominant emissive mechanism: internal controlled torsion snapshots show twist-induced decoupling (oscillator collapse; dark S1 at 90° with first bright shifting to higher state) without energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state, consistent with an ICT/CT-mixed emissive manifold whose emission is suppressed by rotation in solution and restored under restriction (AIE/RIR). Verifier close-family literature specifically reports ICT-AIE behavior (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts) and absence of TICT hallmarks (dual emission; stabilized ~90° S1 minimum), and notes methoxy donors are generally insufficient to yield stabilized TICT states.
